---
title: "Data integrity"
subtitle: "3316712 AI Computational Pharmaceutical Sciences"
author: Natapol Pornputtapong, PhD
institute: Chulalongkorn University
date: today # today | last-modified
date-format: "DD MMM YYYY" # see https://momentjs.com/docs/#/displaying/format/
pdf-engine: xelatex
execute:
    echo: true
format:
    rxquarto-revealjs:
        self-contained: false # set to true to produce a single HTML file
        transition: none # none | fade | slide | convex | concave | zoom
        background-transition: none
        scrollable: true
        code-fold: true
---

## Ethical on Data

::: {.r-fit-text}
“Fabrication 
and 
Falsification"
:::

It is a primary responsibility of a researcher to avoid either a false statement or an omission that distorts the research record. 

A researcher must not report anticipated research results that had not yet been observed at the time of submission of the report.

## Avoid misconduct on data

- Data integrity
- Use and mis-use of data
- Ownership and access of data
- Storage and Retention of Data
- Data privacy

## Data integrity

maintain a **clear and complete record of data** acquired. 
sufficient detail to permit examination for the purpose of replicating the research, responding to questions that may result from unintentional error or misinterpretation, establishing authenticity of the records, and confirming the validity of the conclusions.
destruction of research records or the failure to maintain and produce research records ^[http://www.provost.pitt.edu/documents/GUIDELINES%20FOR%20ETHICAL%20PRACTICES%20IN%20RESEARCH-FINALrevised2-March%202011.pdf
]

## Use and Mis-use

- Negative result data
- Large background
Do not: withholding of information about confounding factors. If some data should be disregarded for a stated reason, confirmed by an approved statistical test for neglecting outliers, the reason should be stated in the published accounts. 

Any intentional or reckless disregard for the truth in reporting observations may be considered to be an act of research misconduct. 

## Data cleansing

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Dirty data**

- Invalid	
- Inaccurate	
- Incomplete	
- Inconsistent	
- Duplicate entries	
- Incorrectly formatted	

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Clean data**

- Valid
- Accurate
- Complete
- Consistent
- Unique
- Uniform

:::

::::


## Example: Data validation

A date of birth as dd-mm-yyyy,

day field will allow numbers up to 31

month field up to 12

year field up to 2021

## Example: Inaccurate data

Survey question:

How often do you go grocery?

- Every day
- Once a week
- Biweekly
- Once a month
- Less than once a month
- Never

Inaccurate number from instruments

## Example: Incomplete data

Missing data

Handling:
- Prevent
- Listwise or case deletion, Pairwise deletion
- Imputation: Mean substitution, Regression imputation, Maximum likelihood, Expectation-Maximization
- Sensitivity analysis
- Last observation carried forward


## Example: Inconsistent data

In your survey about demographic variables, including age, ethnicity, education level, and socioeconomic status.

One participant enters 
“13” for their age
PhD-level education as their highest attained degree

Or

“14” for their age
Have 2 children

## Example: Duplicate entries

Fills out the questionnaire twice
Record twice or multiple time
Subject duplication

## Example: Nonuniform data

In a survey, ask to enter their gross salary in U.S. dollars.
Some respond with their monthly salary
others report their annual salary
Using difference units

## Ownership and access of data

Research data obtained in studies performed at the University and/or by employees of the University are not the property of the researcher who generated or observed them or even of the principal investigator of the research group. They belong to the University, which can be held accountable for the integrity of the data even if the researchers have left the University.

## Storage and Retention of Data

- Stored securely for at least five years after completion
- Deposited in a national or international databank
    - X-ray crystallographic data on protein structures, 
    - human genomic
    - proteomic data
    - DNA microarray data.

## Data privacy

**By Law**

- National Health Act: 2007
- Personal data protection act: 2019

## Data protection

- anonymization
- access control
- avoid share drive

# Data Preprocessing with Tidier.jl

## The Titanic Dataset

[Kaggle Titanic Dataset download](titanic.csv)

**Objective:** Predict whether a passenger survived the Titanic disaster.

**This is a Binary Classification Problem:**
- Target: `Survived` (0 = No, 1 = Yes)

::: {style="font-size:0.65em; "}
| Feature | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `Pclass` | Ordinal | Passenger class (1, 2, 3) |
| `Sex` | Categorical | male / female |
| `Age` | Continuous | Age in years |
| `SibSp` | Integer | # siblings/spouses aboard |
| `Parch` | Integer | # parents/children aboard |
| `Fare` | Continuous | Ticket fare |
| `Embarked` | Categorical | Port (C, Q, S) |
:::

## Tidier.jl

-   `@filter`: Filter rows based on conditions
-   `@select`: Select specific columns
-   `@mutate`: Create or transform columns
-   `@summarize`: Aggregate data (mean, sum, etc.)
-   `@group_by`: Group data for operations
-   `@chain`: Combine multiple steps (from Chain.jl)

## Tidier.jl installation

```julia
using Pkg
Pkg.add("Tidier")
Pkg.add("TidierFiles")
Pkg.add("TidierPlots")
```

# Data Preprocessing with Tidier.jl

## Loading libraries
```{julia}
using Tidier
using TidierFiles
``` 

## Load Data

```{julia}
# Load data
df = read_csv("titanic.csv")

# Inspect
println("Shape: ", size(df))
println("\nFirst 5 rows:")
first(df, 5)
```

## Glimpse of Data

```{julia}
# Summary statistics
@glimpse df
```

## Cleaning up column names
```{julia}
# Clean column names
@chain df begin
    @clean_names()
end
```

## Select specific columns
```{julia}
# Select only Name and Age columns
@chain df begin
    @select(Name, Age)
end
```

## Select column by range
```{julia}
# Select columns from Name to Age
@chain df begin
    @select(Name:Age)
end
```

## Select the first 5 columns individually by number
```{julia}
# Select the first 5 columns
@chain df begin
    @select(1:5)
end
```

## Select columns that start with "P"
```{julia}
# Select columns that start with "P"
@chain df begin
    @select(starts_with("P"))
end
```

## slice data
```{julia}
# Select first 10 rows
@chain df begin
    @slice(1:10)
end
```

## filtering data with conditions

```{julia}
# Filter passengers under 18 years old
@chain df begin
    @filter(Age < 18)
end
```

## Filter passengers in 1st class
```{julia}
@chain df begin
    @filter(Cabin in ["C123", "C124", "C125"])
end
```

## Filter missing values
```{julia}
# Filter out rows with missing Age values
@chain df begin
    @filter(!ismissing(Age))
end
```

## Create new columns
```{julia}
# Create a new column for age group
@chain df begin
    @filter(!ismissing(Age))
    @mutate(AgeGroup = ifelse(Age < 18, "Child", "Adult"))
end
```

## Summarize data
```{julia}
# Calculate average age of passengers
@chain df begin
    @filter(!ismissing(Age))
    @summarize(AvgAge = mean(Age))
end
```

## Summarize more complex statistics
```{julia}
# Calculate average age and fare by passenger class
@chain df begin
    @filter(!ismissing(Age))
    @summarize(AvgAge = mean(Age), MedAge = median(Age))
end
```

## Grouping data
```{julia}
# Calculate average age by passenger class
@chain df begin
    @filter(!ismissing(Age))
    @group_by(Pclass)
    @summarize(AvgAge = mean(Age))
end
```

## Grouping by multiple columns
```{julia}
# Calculate average age by passenger class and  gender
@chain df begin
    @filter(!ismissing(Age))
    @group_by(Pclass, Sex)
    @summarize(AvgAge = mean(Age))
end
```

## Arranging data
```{julia}
# Arrange passengers by age in descending order
@chain df begin
    @filter(!ismissing(Age))
    @arrange(Age)
end
```
## Arranging data descending
```{julia}
# Arrange passengers by age in descending order
@chain df begin
    @filter(!ismissing(Age))
    @arrange(desc(Age))
end
```

## Arranging by multiple columns
```{julia}
# Arrange passengers by class and then age
@chain df begin
    @filter(!ismissing(Age))
    @arrange(Pclass, Age)
end
```

## Distinct values
```{julia}
# Get distinct passenger classes
@chain df begin
    @distinct(Pclass)
end
```

# Data visualization with TidierPlots.jl

## load TidierPlots
```{julia}
using TidierPlots
```

## Bar plot of passenger class distribution
```{julia}
# Bar plot of passenger class distribution
@chain df begin
    ggplot(@aes(x = Pclass))
        geom_bar()
end
```

## Histogram of passenger ages
```{julia}
# Histogram of passenger ages
@chain df begin
    ggplot(@aes(x = Age))
        geom_histogram(binwidth = 5)
end
```

## Box plot of fare by passenger class
```{julia}
# Box plot of fare by passenger class
@chain df begin
    ggplot(@aes(x = Pclass, y = Fare))
        geom_boxplot()
end
```

## Scatter plot of age vs fare
```{julia}
# Scatter plot of age vs fare
@chain df begin
    ggplot(@aes(x = Age, y = Fare))
        geom_point()
end
```

## Scatter plot of age vs fare colored by survival
```{julia}
# Scatter plot of age vs fare colored by survival
@chain df begin
    ggplot(@aes(x = Age, y = Fare, color = Survived))
        geom_point()
end
```

## smooth scatter plot of age vs fare colored by survival
```{julia}
#| warning: false

# Smooth scatter plot of age vs fare colored by survival
@chain df begin
    ggplot(@aes(x = Age, y = Fare, color = Survived))
        geom_point()
        geom_smooth(method = "loess")
end
```

## tile plot of survival rate by age group and passenger class
```julia
# Create age groups
@chain df begin
    @filter(!ismissing(Age))
    @mutate(AgeGroup = ifelse(Age < 18, 0, 1))
    @group_by(AgeGroup, Pclass)
    @summarize(SurvivalRate = mean(Survived))
    ggplot(@aes(x = AgeGroup, y = Pclass, z = SurvivalRate))
        geom_tile()
end
```
